speaker: interviewer
text: The result of the wartime experience, causing some people to go crazy-- for yourself, it means it affects your sleep. It means that, sometimes, you need to take medication. Do you ever have dreams about your experiences through the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I haven't got these dreams. I have problems sleeping, which I had for the last 40-odd years. I've been on all sort of sleeping tablets. And it's true that I have, sometimes, terrible dreams, but that's not why it is. I can't sleep.

speaker: interviewee
text: So going to all sorts of the doctors. And the tablet which I'm taking now, called Rohypnol. According to my general practitioner, it's going to ruin my life. So I'm trying to get out of it. What is your greatest pleasure, today, at this point of your life? My greatest pleasure is my family. Yes, and my grandchildren. And a bit of excitement to give some money away. Right? To be charitable. I'm not very, very wealthy, in comparison to some of the people which I know. I'm looking at Nicole. And-- but at the same time, I'm trying to do my best for the Jewish Communal Appeal, for united-- it is appeal for all the universities in Israel, for also the community in general, not only the Jewish one, but everything else. And I-- it gives me a lot of pleasure to do that. My name-- you know, I am not a covert mensch. You know what covert is? My grandchildren or children will see that, or my nephews. Covert means honor. It doesn't go with me. But I must-- I must say, to have my name as a yeshiva with Central Synagogue-- which burned down-- with the Emanuel School, and with many other-- and with Moranbah Hospital-- gives me a lot of pleasure. Right? Now, how do you feel about being a Jew? I feel wonderful. I feel wonderful. I feel on top of the world, to be that. Paul, is there anything else special that you would like to tell us?

speaker: interviewee
text: Anything special? Yes. Can I think about that for a minute? Yes. I-- I would say that-- and I am, at the moment, by myself. I would like to meet a very nice Jewish woman, because I'm living by myself, which has got [YIDDISH], that you wouldn't understand, a pleasantness, and being herself, no showing off. I wouldn't mind having that.

speaker: interviewee
text: As a Jewish man, I would like to see Israel being strong and not being worried about their neighbors. Thank you, very much. I think this is it, isn't it? Paul, thank you very, very much, for sharing your very special, very extraordinary story with us, all your experience of the war, being one of the liberators, being there for others, being a father, being a benefactor, and for giving so much of yourself as a leader, and surviving such a difficult experience. It's been an honor to speak with you. Thank you. Who's in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is myself and Melitta.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: The photo was taken in Guelph, Ontario.

speaker: interviewer
text: Around about what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: That would have been around May, June, 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Tell me about this photo. Who is it?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is myself and Melitta.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken in Marseille.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year would that have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1942. And that's the way we escaped, with our coat, hat, and the way we look like. Right? There's no-- no-- no difference, OK? Anyway, that's the way it is.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about this photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo has been taken in Scheveningen, before we left Holland, in 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's yourself and Melitta?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's myself and Melitta.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Tell-- tell about this photo, Paul.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo is on top of my [? Benford ?] 1,500 weight, in France. I can't tell you where it was. It could have been around Normandy.

speaker: interviewer
text: When would that have been taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That would have been-- Normandy was 19-- was in-- would have been May, June 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Thank you. [? Give me ?] one minute.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you see that? So this was 1943, this picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: This would be in April-- March, April 1943.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's in the photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Jamaica. This is Melitta and myself enjoying ourself. I mean, I hope we enjoyed-- I mean, I know we enjoyed ourself. And that's us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Thank You.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who are these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is Charles [? Gotfried. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: On the left, or right?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the right. A Colonel in the American Army. Born in Holland, and with myself. He visit me in the camp in Dovercourt. He was a wonderful friend to myself and towards my children, in particular, to Anita, when she went to San Francisco. He lived in San Francisco. He didn't want to marry anymore, because of his family, which disappeared completely.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo was taken in 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Paul, is this you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about this photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: This photo--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was taken in London.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were eating a pineapple?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was the first pineapple I ever tasted. And it was after the liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, very much. Tell me about these documents.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was my Dutch passport, organized by the Dutch honorary consul, [? Monsieur Jacques, ?] in Lyon, by the Swedish consulate in Vichy. With the exception of one or two, all these visas, visa de sortie, et cetera, et cetera, were all forgeries, which were produced in the toilet, where we had our printery, in Hotel Select in Marseille. The only one which was genuine was the Liberian visa, which I can't just pick at this moment. Your photographer, or your-- Anyway, it is fine. It's good. And just turn this around, please. And you see me. And-- and-- and-- and it's fine. It's good. And all this is, with the-- even including the visa de sortie, including the salida, they were all forgeries. Thank you, very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewer
text: What are these books?

speaker: interviewee
text: These are-- [COUGHING] it-- we-- is it already going, or not? These are my soldiers book. When I joined the Army-- OK, can I put them up, or not?

speaker: interviewer
text: These are when you joined the Army, Paul?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I joined the Army in Guelph, Ontario, that was the first soldiers book which they gave me. The other one, I don't know it was before, or afterwards, is there. And I somehow, kept it in my-- in my-- you know, in my drawer. And that's what it is. And is there anything else you want me to tell you?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, that's great.

speaker: interviewee
text: I must tell you something. It is the commander training, as young as I was, wasn't an easy one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were the youngest serviceman?

speaker: interviewee
text: Officer.

speaker: interviewer
text: The youngest officer.

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in the Royal Dutch Brigade Princess Irene. But that came in-- after Normandy, right? But that has got nothing to do with it, really.

words.text: nothing to do with it, really.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Thanks. Tell me about this certificate.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got this only recently. I've got 14 medals from France, from Belgium, and from Holland. And I just got them. And and-- I don't-- I don't think that I've been a hero.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in Normandy, on D-Day?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was at D-Day, Normandy, with 32 men, which I mentioned before. And I would say what got me through it was sheer luck. Sheer luck stands for mazel, because I saw some people dying around me, and I wouldn't be able to take that anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you, very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right?

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about this news article.

speaker: interviewee
text: This news article is when-- when Johannes Louwers was liberated, or liberated, but out of prison from Czechoslovakia, which I have to read it. I think it was in 1951. He was 19 months in jail, first being sentenced to death, being switched over to life sentence, and by the Dutch government putting an embargo toward Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the two others, which I mentioned before, Sanderst and Vogeler, which was England and America doing the same thing. It was liberated. And all three of them came out, more or less, at the same time. You see, in this photo, Johannes Louwers. You see myself there, right? And I was very happy to have him with me. The name Kolotex comes from Kornmehl, Louwers Textiles. Right? Thank you very much. How many medals are there here?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know how many medals. Altogether, I think that I've got 14. They are from France, from Belgium, and from Holland. I don't think that I've got any from England. That doesn't really give me that satisfaction. It makes me sad to think of my comrades, of the guys which were with me in the brigade which died, and which lost either an eye or an arm, which are not old now. They are not with us anymore. And that's what it is all about.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I would say I've been very lucky that I've still got everything going with me. But I'm not-- I wasn't a hero, I was just lucky. Right? Thank you, very much, Paul.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
